teachings.teaching: The Lord's touch purifies even the most sinful, as seen when Krishna's foot touched the serpent and freed him from a curse.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates that divine contact (through mantra or guru's grace) instantly removes karmic impurities, leading to liberation.

teachings.teaching: The name of Krishna purifies both the speaker and the listener.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that chanting the divine name (nama-japa) is a powerful Kriya that cleanses the mind and accelerates spiritual progress.

teachings.teaching: Devotees should surrender to Krishna for protection from all fears.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga principle of complete surrender (prapatti) as the means to overcome worldly anxieties and attain peace.

chapter_summary: The Gopis describe Kṛṣṇa's flute playing and its enchanting effect on all beings in Vṛndāvana, including themselves, the cows, birds, rivers, trees, and even the gods. They express their intense longing and love for Kṛṣṇa.

story.title: The Gopis' Song of Kṛṣṇa's Flute
story.arc: Kṛṣṇa's Vṛndāvana pastimes
story.timeline_note: During Kṛṣṇa's childhood in Vṛndāvana, when He goes to the forest with the cows.
story.comic_potential: The humorous descriptions of the cows and deer being mesmerized by Kṛṣṇa's flute, and the Gopis' exaggerated expressions of longing.

teachings.teaching: The divine sound of Kṛṣṇa's flute captivates all beings, drawing them to spiritual absorption.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, the flute represents the inner sound (nāda) that leads to samādhi. The Gopis' absorption mirrors the yogi's focus on the subtle sound.

teachings.teaching: Even inanimate nature responds to Kṛṣṇa's presence, showing the non-dual nature of reality.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would see this as the manifestation of consciousness in all forms; the trees and rivers are not separate from the divine.

teachings.teaching: The Gopis' love for Kṛṣṇa exemplifies the highest devotion (prema-bhakti), where one forgets all worldly attachments.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with Kriya-Yoga's emphasis on surrendering the ego and merging with the divine through love.

teachings.teaching: Kṛṣṇa's flute playing is a form of divine teaching, instructing all beings through sound.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's commentary would highlight that the flute's melody is the guru's teaching, awakening the soul.

chapter_summary: The demon Ariṣṭa, in the form of a bull, terrorizes Vraja. Kṛṣṇa kills him, then Nārada informs Kaṃsa that Kṛṣṇa and Balarāma are the sons of Vasudeva and Devakī, destined to kill him. Kaṃsa imprisons Vasudeva and Devakī, sends Keśī to kill the boys, and plots to have them killed by the elephant Kuvalayāpīḍa and wrestlers. He sends Akrūra to bring them to Mathurā for a bow sacrifice.

story.title: The Killing of Ariṣṭa and Kaṃsa's Plot
story.arc: Kṛṣṇa's Vṛndāvana pastimes and the prelude to Mathurā
story.timeline_note: After the killing of Ariṣṭa, Nārada reveals Kṛṣṇa's identity to Kaṃsa, leading to Kaṃsa's plot.
story.comic_potential: High: dramatic bull demon fight, Nārada's revelation, Kaṃsa's rage, and the setup for the Mathurā showdown.

teachings.teaching: The Lord protects His devotees and destroys the wicked.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this represents the inner purification where the divine force removes obstacles (demons) that hinder spiritual progress.

teachings.teaching: One should not be attached to the results of actions; success and failure depend on destiny.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes equanimity in action, aligning with Akrūra's advice to Kaṃsa that results are in the hands of fate.

chapter_summary: Krishna kills the demon Kesi (in horse form) and later the demon Vyoma (son of Maya), rescuing cowherd boys. The sage Narada visits Krishna, praises him, and prophesies his future exploits.

story.title: Killing of Kesi and Vyoma, and Narada's Prophecy
story.arc: Krishna's childhood pastimes in Vrindavan and Dvaraka
story.timeline_note: After Kesi's death, Narada visits Krishna and foretells future events including the killing of Kamsa and other demons, and the Mahabharata war.
story.comic_potential: High: dramatic battles with a horse-demon and a shape-shifting demon, playful cowherd boys, and a sage's prophecy.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is the inner controller and witness of all beings, residing in the heart.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this points to the practice of turning inward to realize the Self as the witness (sakshi) beyond the mind.

teachings.teaching: The Lord creates the universe through His maya and then enters it as the indwelling Self.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that the yogi must transcend maya by identifying with the pure consciousness that is the substratum of all creation.

teachings.teaching: The Lord descends to protect the righteous and destroy the wicked.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga view that divine grace manifests to remove obstacles on the spiritual path.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is pure consciousness, free from the flow of maya's gunas.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma teaches that through Kriya-Yoga, one can realize this state of nitya-nivritta-maya, where the gunas no longer bind.

chapter_summary: Akrūra travels from Mathurā to Gokula, reflecting on his good fortune to see Kṛṣṇa and Balarāma. Upon arrival, he worships their footprints, meets them, and is warmly received by Nanda and the brothers. The chapter highlights Akrūra's devotion and the divine nature of Kṛṣṇa and Balarāma.

story.title: Akrūra's Journey to Gokula
story.arc: Akrūra travels to Gokula, meets Kṛṣṇa and Balarāma, and is received with hospitality.
story.timeline_note: After the night in Mathurā, Akrūra travels to Gokula and arrives at sunset.
story.comic_potential: Akrūra's internal monologue of devotion and his emotional prostration before Kṛṣṇa and Balarāma.

teachings.teaching: Seeing the Lord is extremely rare and is the result of great merit.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the importance of divine grace and the culmination of spiritual practice.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is impartial but reciprocates with devotees according to their devotion.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework highlights the principle of 'bhakti' as the key to divine connection, where the Lord responds to the devotee's love.

teachings.teaching: The purpose of human life is to abandon pride, fear, and sorrow, and to engage in seeing, hearing, and serving the Lord.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with Kriya-Yoga's emphasis on direct experience and service as means to transcend the ego.

chapter_summary: Akrura arrives in Vrindavan to take Krishna and Balarama to Mathura for the festival. The gopis lament the impending separation from Krishna, expressing their deep love and devotion. Krishna and Balarama depart with Akrura, and the gopis are left in sorrow. On the way, they stop at the Yamuna, where Akrura performs ablutions and has a vision of the cosmic form of the Lord.

story.title: Akrura's Arrival and the Gopis' Lament
story.arc: Krishna's departure from Vrindavan to Mathura
story.timeline_note: Occurs after Krishna's childhood pastimes in Vrindavan, before the killing of Kamsa.
story.comic_potential: The emotional contrast between the gopis' intense sorrow and Akrura's cosmic vision at the Yamuna offers dramatic and visual storytelling opportunities.

teachings.teaching: The gopis' intense love for Krishna illustrates the pinnacle of devotion (bhakti), where separation (viraha) intensifies longing and spiritual realization.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this viraha-bhakti is the highest form of yoga, where the soul's yearning for the Divine becomes the driving force for inner transformation and union.

teachings.teaching: Akrura's vision of the cosmic form of the Lord in the Yamuna reveals that the divine is both immanent and transcendent, accessible through sincere practice.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes that through disciplined meditation (dhyana) and purification (snanam), one can perceive the divine presence within and without, as Akrura did.

chapter_summary: Akrura, having seen Krishna and Balarama, is overwhelmed with devotion. He offers a prayer describing Krishna's divine form, associates, and attributes, and bows down in reverence.

story.title: Akrura's Prayer to Krishna
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes in Dvaraka
story.timeline_note: After Krishna and Balarama's arrival in Mathura, Akrura sees them and offers this prayer.
story.comic_potential: Akrura's ecstatic devotion, with hair standing on end and tear-filled eyes, could be depicted humorously as an overwhelmed devotee struggling to speak.

teachings.teaching: The divine form of Krishna is adorned with the Srivatsa mark, Kaustubha gem, and a garland of forest flowers, and is served by many associates and personified energies.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this vision represents the culmination of devotional practice where the practitioner perceives the Lord's transcendental form through purified senses.

teachings.teaching: Akrura's prayer is offered with intense love, his body hair standing on end and eyes filled with tears, speaking in a choked voice.
teachings.lens_note: This exemplifies the Kriya-Yoga state of bhava-samadhi, where the devotee's emotions become a direct expression of union with the divine.

chapter_summary: Akrūra offers a profound prayer to Kṛṣṇa, acknowledging Him as the supreme cause of all causes, the source of the universe, and the ultimate object of all forms of worship. He describes the cosmic form of the Lord, enumerates His avatāras, and confesses his own ignorance and bondage to material existence, seeking refuge at Kṛṣṇa's lotus feet.

story.title: Akrūra's Prayer to Kṛṣṇa
story.arc: Akrūra, having arrived in Vṛndāvana, offers a heartfelt prayer to Kṛṣṇa, acknowledging His supremacy and seeking refuge.
story.timeline_note: This chapter occurs during Kṛṣṇa's pastimes in Vṛndāvana, before the Kurukshetra war.
story.comic_potential: Akrūra's ecstatic prayer and the enumeration of avatāras could be visually depicted with cosmic imagery.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is the ultimate cause of all causes, and everything emanates from Him.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to the realization of the Supreme Self as the source of all prāṇa and consciousness, the foundation of all yogic practices.

teachings.teaching: All forms of worship, whether Vedic, yogic, or devotional, ultimately reach the same Supreme Lord.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that Kriya-Yoga integrates all paths, recognizing that the goal is one, though methods differ.

teachings.teaching: The material world is a manifestation of the Lord's energy, and the living entities are entangled in māyā, identifying with the body and possessions.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga teaching that the mind must be purified through meditation to overcome the false identification with the body and senses.

teachings.teaching: The only way to liberation is to surrender to the Lord, who is the inner witness and controller.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga stresses that surrender (prapatti) is essential, as the practice of kriya leads to the realization of the Lord as the inner Self.

chapter_summary: Krishna and Balarama, accompanied by Akrura, enter Mathura. They are greeted by the city's residents, especially the women who are captivated by Krishna's beauty. Krishna kills a washerman who refuses to give him garments, then receives fine clothes from a tailor. He and Balarama visit the florist Sudama, who offers them garlands and receives blessings. The chapter highlights Krishna's divine charm and his interactions with various people.

story.title: Krishna and Balarama Enter Mathura
story.arc: Krishna's journey to Mathura and his early interactions with its inhabitants
story.timeline_note: Occurs after Krishna and Balarama leave Vrindavan and before the killing of Kamsa.
story.comic_potential: High: The washerman's arrogance and swift punishment, the tailor's eager service, and the women's infatuation provide vivid, dramatic scenes.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is impartial and bestows grace on those who serve Him with devotion, regardless of their social status.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the principle of 'Ishvara-pranidhana' (surrender to God) where the devotee's attitude determines the response, not external position.

teachings.teaching: Material possessions and pride lead to downfall; humility and devotion bring divine blessings.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework emphasizes 'vairagya' (detachment) and 'abhyasa' (practice); the washerman's attachment to his position leads to death, while the tailor's humble service earns grace.

teachings.teaching: The divine presence captivates the hearts of all beings, drawing them towards spiritual realization.
teachings.lens_note: This reflects the Kriya-Yoga concept of 'pratyahara' (withdrawal of senses) as the women's minds are absorbed in Krishna, transcending mundane concerns.

chapter_summary: This chapter describes the aftermath of Krishna's blessing to Sudarshan, where he grants him a boon of devotion to his devotees, compassion for all beings, and prosperity that increases his lineage. After giving the boon, Krishna and Balarama depart.

story.title: Krishna's Blessing to Sudarshan
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes in Dvaraka
story.timeline_note: After the events of the previous chapter where Sudarshan worshipped Krishna.
story.comic_potential: A scene of Krishna bestowing a boon with a gentle smile, Sudarshan bowing in gratitude, and Balarama standing nearby.

teachings.teaching: Devotion to Krishna's devotees and compassion for all beings are the highest virtues.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the practice of selfless service (seva) and universal compassion as essential for spiritual progress.

teachings.teaching: The boon includes prosperity that enhances one's lineage, indicating that material blessings follow spiritual devotion.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework sees this as the natural result of aligning one's actions with dharma, leading to both inner and outer abundance.

chapter_summary: Krishna and Balarama enter Mathura, encounter the hunchbacked woman Trivakra who offers them sandalwood paste, and Krishna straightens her back. They then proceed to the bow ceremony, where Krishna breaks the great bow of Kamsa, kills the guards, and later returns to their camp. Kamsa, hearing of these events, becomes terrified and sees omens of death. He arranges a wrestling match the next day, inviting Nanda and the cowherds.

story.title: Krishna and Balarama in Mathura: The Bow Breaking and Omens of Kamsa
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes in Mathura leading to the killing of Kamsa
story.timeline_note: This chapter occurs after Krishna and Balarama arrive in Mathura and before the wrestling match where Kamsa is killed.
story.comic_potential: High: The encounter with Trivakra is humorous and tender; the breaking of the bow is dramatic; Kamsa's nightmares and omens are visually striking.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's touch can transform and elevate even the most lowly and crooked, as seen when Krishna straightens Trivakra's back and makes her beautiful.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this symbolizes how divine grace (kriya) can straighten the crooked tendencies of the mind (vrittis) and awaken spiritual beauty.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is not bound by material rules; He acts with spontaneous power (urukrama) and breaks the bow of pride (symbolized by Kamsa's bow).
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga interprets this as the breaking of the ego (ahamkara) through the practice of kriya, which dismantles the false sense of doership.

teachings.teaching: Kamsa's fear and omens illustrate how a sinful mind perceives death everywhere; the Lord's presence brings terror to the wicked.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this represents the state of a restless mind (kshipta) that sees obstacles everywhere, while the yogi sees the Lord's grace.

chapter_summary: Krishna and Balarama, having heard the wrestling arena's drum, go to see it. At the entrance, Krishna confronts and kills the elephant Kuvalayapida, then enters the arena with Balarama. The people rejoice, recognizing them as incarnations of Narayana. The wrestler Canura challenges them to a match.

story.title: Krishna and Balarama Enter the Arena
story.arc: Kamsa's downfall
story.timeline_note: After the killing of various demons in Vrindavan, Krishna and Balarama go to Mathura for the wrestling festival.
story.comic_potential: High: dramatic elephant fight, crowd reactions, and the challenge of the wrestlers.

teachings.teaching: One should act in a way that pleases the king, as subjects find welfare by pleasing the king, who is the embodiment of all beings.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this reflects the principle of aligning individual actions with the cosmic order (dharma), where the king represents the divine will. Pleasing the king symbolizes harmonizing with the universal flow.

teachings.teaching: Even in competition, one should ensure that righteousness (dharma) is not violated.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that Kriya-Yoga is not about mere physical prowess but about maintaining inner purity and ethical conduct. Krishna's insistence on fair play exemplifies the yogic principle of non-violence (ahimsa) in action.

chapter_summary: Krishna and Balarama defeat and kill the wrestlers Canura, Mustika, Kuta, Sala, and Tosala in the Mathura arena, then Krishna kills Kamsa. The women of Kamsa's family lament, and Krishna and Balarama release their parents from bondage.

story.title: Krishna and Balarama Kill Kamsa's Wrestlers and Kamsa
story.arc: Kamsa's death and liberation of parents
story.timeline_note: After the wrestling match, Krishna kills Kamsa on the same day.
story.comic_potential: High; dramatic wrestling match, divine intervention, and a tyrant's downfall.

teachings.teaching: The Lord protects the righteous and destroys the wicked; even in a seemingly unfair fight, divine justice prevails.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this represents the inner battle where the practitioner, through disciplined action (kriya), overcomes the wrestlers (sensory distractions) and the tyrant (ego) to realize the Self.

teachings.teaching: One should not associate with a gathering where adharma (unrighteousness) is allowed to flourish; the wise avoid such assemblies.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that the practitioner must avoid environments that disturb the mind and hinder spiritual progress.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is the ultimate refuge and the cause of all beings; those who oppose Him find no peace.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this underscores the necessity of surrendering the ego to the divine will, as resistance leads to suffering.

chapter_summary: Krishna and Balarama return to Mathura, reunite with their parents Vasudeva and Devaki, and console them. They install Ugrasena as king, welcome back the Yadava clans who had fled due to Kamsa, and send Nanda and the gopas back to Vrindavan with gifts. Krishna and Balarama undergo their sacred thread ceremony with Garga as teacher, then study under Sandipani in Avanti, learning all Vedas, Upanishads, Dhanurveda, and other sciences in 64 days. As guru-dakshina, they retrieve Sandipani's son from the demon Panchajana (who had taken the form of a conch) and from Yama's city, restoring the boy to life.

story.title: Krishna and Balarama's Return to Mathura and Education
story.arc: Krishna's early life and education
story.timeline_note: After Kamsa's death, before the Kurukshetra war.
story.comic_potential: The retrieval of Sandipani's son from the ocean and Yama's city has high adventure potential.

teachings.teaching: One who does not support parents, wife, children, guru, or dependents is considered dead while breathing.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the duty of seva (selfless service) as a foundation for spiritual growth.

teachings.teaching: The guru's instruction is to be followed with devotion; the guru-dakshina is a means to express gratitude.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga stresses the importance of guru-seva and the completion of obligations as part of sadhana.

chapter_summary: Uddhava is sent by Krishna to Vrindavan to console Nanda, Yashoda, and the gopis who are suffering from separation. Uddhava delivers Krishna's message, describes his divine nature, and assures them of his return. The chapter ends with the gopis' morning activities and their curiosity about Uddhava's arrival.

story.title: Uddhava's Visit to Vrindavan
story.arc: Krishna's separation and Uddhava's consolation
story.timeline_note: After Krishna's departure to Mathura, before the Kurukshetra war.
story.comic_potential: The emotional reunion and the gopis' curiosity about Uddhava's chariot.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is the inner self of all beings, and one should see Him everywhere.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to the realization of the inner Self (Atman) as the witness consciousness, achieved through meditation and pranayama.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is beyond duality, without friends or enemies, and acts only for the protection of the righteous.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes transcending dualities through the practice of equanimity and devotion.

teachings.teaching: The Lord, though unaffected by the modes of nature, appears to engage in them for the sake of creation, preservation, and destruction.
teachings.lens_note: This reflects the Kriya-Yoga understanding of the gunas as energies that can be transcended through inner stillness.

teachings.teaching: The mind creates the illusion of doership; the self is the witness.
teachings.lens_note: A core teaching of Kriya-Yoga: through meditation, one realizes the self as the silent witness, not the doer.

teachings.teaching: Krishna is the father, mother, and Lord of all beings.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this is the realization of the divine as the ultimate source and support of all existence.

chapter_summary: Uddhava visits the gopis of Vrindavan as a messenger from Krishna. The gopis express their intense separation and love for Krishna, questioning his faithfulness. Uddhava delivers Krishna's message, which includes philosophical teachings on the nature of the self and the importance of constant remembrance. The gopis, though comforted, remain immersed in thoughts of Krishna.

story.title: Uddhava's Visit to the Gopis
story.arc: Krishna's separation and the gopis' devotion
story.timeline_note: After Krishna's departure to Mathura and Dwarka, before the Kurukshetra war.
story.comic_potential: The gopis' dramatic lamentations and Uddhava's attempts to console them provide emotional and humorous contrasts.

teachings.teaching: The self (atman) is pure consciousness, distinct from the body and mind, and is unaffected by the three states of waking, dreaming, and deep sleep.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes direct realization of the self through meditation, transcending the modifications of the mind.

teachings.teaching: One should control the senses and withdraw the mind from sense objects, like one who is awake controls dreams.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the practice of pratyahara (sense withdrawal) in Kriya-Yoga, a key step to inner stillness.

teachings.teaching: All spiritual practices (yoga, sankhya, charity, austerity, etc.) culminate in knowledge of the self, just as rivers merge into the ocean.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga integrates these paths, emphasizing that the ultimate goal is self-realization through disciplined practice.

teachings.teaching: Separation from the beloved (Krishna) is a means to intensify remembrance and love, as the mind focuses more on an absent beloved than a present one.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, the practice of constant remembrance (smarana) is a powerful technique to unite the mind with the divine, turning separation into a catalyst for deeper connection.

teachings.teaching: By fixing the mind entirely on Krishna and remembering him constantly, one will soon attain him.
teachings.lens_note: This is the essence of bhakti-yoga, which Kriya-Yoga supports through meditative absorption in the divine form.

teachings.teaching: The highest happiness is detachment (nairashya), as exemplified by the prostitute Pingala.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga teaches that true contentment arises from inner detachment, not from external objects.

chapter_summary: Uddhava visits Vrindavan to console the gopis and Nanda's family after Krishna's departure. He stays for several months, singing Krishna's pastimes and alleviating their sorrow. The gopis' intense love for Krishna is praised, and Uddhava returns to Mathura, conveying their devotion to Krishna.

story.title: Uddhava's Mission to Vrindavan
story.arc: Uddhava consoles the gopis and returns to Mathura
story.timeline_note: After Krishna's departure to Mathura, before the Kurukshetra war.
story.comic_potential: Uddhava's attempt to console the lovesick gopis, who are oblivious to his philosophical teachings, could be played for gentle humor.

teachings.teaching: The gopis' love for Krishna is the highest form of devotion, surpassing even the desires of great sages and yogis.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the pinnacle of bhakti-yoga where the devotee's entire being is absorbed in the divine, transcending all worldly attachments and even scriptural injunctions.

teachings.teaching: Even those who are seemingly unqualified (like the gopis) can attain the highest spiritual perfection through pure devotion.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that Kriya-Yoga is accessible to all, regardless of external purity or social status; the gopis exemplify this by their unwavering love.

teachings.teaching: The dust of the feet of the gopis is supremely purifying, and their songs about Krishna sanctify the three worlds.
teachings.lens_note: This highlights the power of satsanga (association with devotees) and kirtana (chanting divine names), core practices in Kriya-Yoga.

teachings.teaching: The gopis' prayer: may our mind, speech, and body be always engaged in Krishna's service.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga ideal of offering all actions (karma) to the divine, transforming daily life into a continuous yoga.

chapter_summary: Krishna visits the courtesan's house, grants her a boon, then visits Akrura. Akrura praises Krishna and is sent to Hastinapura to check on the Pandavas.

story.title: Krishna's Visit to Akrura and the Courtesan
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes in Dvaraka
story.timeline_note: After Kamsa's death, before the Kurukshetra war.
story.comic_potential: Krishna's visit to the courtesan and his playful interaction with Akrura.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is the supreme controller and the cause of all causes.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: Recognizing the Lord as the inner witness (sakshi) and the ultimate reality behind all actions.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is not bound by the modes of nature or their actions.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: The practitioner should identify with the atman, which is beyond the gunas, and perform actions without attachment.

teachings.teaching: The Lord incarnates to protect dharma and destroy the wicked.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: The descent of the Lord (avatara) is a model for the practitioner to manifest divine qualities in daily life.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's devotees are superior to holy places and deities.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: Association with saintly persons (satsanga) is the most effective means for spiritual progress.

chapter_summary: Akrūra visits Hastinapura, meets with Dhṛtarāṣṭra, Bhīṣma, Vidura, Kuntī, and others, observes the Kauravas' hostility, and delivers a message of peace and dharma to Dhṛtarāṣṭra. Kuntī laments her plight and prays to Kṛṣṇa. Akrūra returns to Dvārakā and reports to Rāma and Kṛṣṇa.

story.title: Akrūra's Mission to Hastinapura
story.arc: Pre-Kurukshetra diplomacy
story.timeline_note: After the Pāṇḍavas' exile, before the Kurukshetra war.
story.comic_potential: Akrūra's diplomatic visit, Kuntī's emotional outburst, Dhṛtarāṣṭra's stubbornness.

teachings.teaching: One is born alone and dies alone; one enjoys the fruits of one's own actions.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the individual nature of karma and the need for personal spiritual effort (sādhana) to attain liberation.

teachings.teaching: Wealth acquired by adharma is taken away by others; the ignorant person who accumulates it is abandoned by his wealth, life, and family.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's commentary would highlight that attachment to material gains (artha) without dharma leads to bondage; true wealth is spiritual merit.

teachings.teaching: This world is like a dream, illusion, and mental construct; one should control the mind and become peaceful.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with Kriya-Yoga's goal of stilling the mind (citta vṛtti nirodha) to realize the true Self beyond māyā.

teachings.teaching: No one can change the Lord's will; He descends to relieve the earth's burden.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would see this as the divine play (līlā) of the avatāra, which the yogi can perceive through inner vision.

teachings.teaching: The Lord creates the world through His inscrutable māyā, enters it, and distributes the guṇas; He is the controller of the cycle of saṃsāra.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, understanding māyā is key to transcending it; the yogi seeks to realize the Parameśvara within.

chapter_summary: Jarāsandha, king of Magadha, attacks Mathurā with a vast army to avenge his son-in-law Kaṃsa's death. Kṛṣṇa and Balarāma, understanding their divine purpose to relieve the earth's burden, engage in battle. After a fierce war, Balarāma captures Jarāsandha but Kṛṣṇa spares him, knowing he will return. Jarāsandha attacks seventeen more times, each time defeated. Before the eighteenth battle, the Yavana king Kālayavana, sent by Nārada, besieges Mathurā. Kṛṣṇa builds a fortress city in the sea to protect the Yādavas.

story.title: Jarāsandha's Attacks and the Building of Dvārakā
story.arc: Kṛṣṇa's early life and establishment of Dvārakā
story.timeline_note: After Kaṃsa's death, Jarāsandha attacks Mathurā seventeen times, then Kālayavana appears before the eighteenth battle.
story.comic_potential: High; dramatic battles, divine weapons, and strategic retreat.

teachings.teaching: The Lord incarnates to relieve the earth's burden and protect the righteous.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to the descent of divine grace to remove obstacles on the spiritual path.

teachings.teaching: A true hero does not boast but demonstrates prowess through action.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that Kriya-Yoga practice is about inner transformation, not outward show.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's actions, though seemingly human, are divine play (līlā).
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, all actions of the realized soul are spontaneous and free from ego.

chapter_summary: This chapter describes the construction of Dvaraka city by the Yadus, the gifts from the gods (Sudharma assembly hall, Parijata tree, horses, treasures), and Krishna's departure from the city with Rama to meet the enemy, unarmed and garlanded.

story.title: The Gifts of the Gods and Krishna's Departure
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes in Dvaraka
story.timeline_note: After the construction of Dvaraka, before the battle with Jarasandha.
story.comic_potential: The gods showering gifts and Krishna walking out unarmed with a garland, showing confidence.

teachings.teaching: The divine gifts (Sudharma, Parijata) grant freedom from mortal limitations.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, these represent the fruits of yogic practice that transcend worldly conditioning.

teachings.teaching: Krishna returns all gifts to the gods, showing detachment.
teachings.lens_note: This illustrates the Kriya-Yoga principle of offering the results of actions back to the divine source.

teachings.teaching: Krishna goes unarmed, trusting in divine protection.
teachings.lens_note: Symbolizes the Kriya-Yoga state of surrender and inner strength beyond external weapons.

chapter_summary: Mucukunda, a great king from the Ikshvaku dynasty, had been granted a boon by the gods to sleep in a cave after a long war. When the Yavana king pursued Krishna into the cave, Mucukunda awoke and burned the Yavana with his gaze. Krishna then revealed himself to Mucukunda, who recognized him as Narayana and offered prayers of surrender.

story.title: Mucukunda's Deliverance
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes in Mathura and Dvaraka
story.timeline_note: After Krishna killed Kamsa and before establishing Dvaraka.
story.comic_potential: The Yavana's chase and Mucukunda's fiery gaze are visually striking.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's names and deeds are infinite and cannot be fully enumerated.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this points to the infinite nature of divine consciousness accessible through meditation.

teachings.teaching: The Lord descends to protect dharma and destroy the demoniac.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga sees this as the inner descent of awareness to purify the mind of negative tendencies.

teachings.teaching: Human life is rare and should be used for devotion, not material entanglement.
teachings.lens_note: This echoes the Kriya-Yoga emphasis on using the human body for spiritual practice to attain liberation.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is the inner controller who awards the fruits of one's actions.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to the witnessing consciousness that observes karma and grants grace.

chapter_summary: King Mucukunda, after being awakened by Kṛṣṇa, recounts his past lives and the futility of worldly pleasures. He expresses desire only for Kṛṣṇa's lotus feet and liberation. Kṛṣṇa praises his unwavering devotion and grants him boons: unalloyed devotion, absolution from sins of hunting, and in the next life, becoming a brāhmaṇa and attaining Him.

story.title: Mucukunda's Liberation
story.arc: Mucukunda, after a long sleep, meets Kṛṣṇa and renounces worldly desires, receiving Kṛṣṇa's grace and promise of liberation.
story.timeline_note: After Kṛṣṇa killed Kālayavana and Mucukunda was awakened.
story.comic_potential: Mucukunda's dramatic awakening and his philosophical discourse with Kṛṣṇa, followed by Kṛṣṇa's boons.

teachings.teaching: Worldly pleasures and royal opulence are temporary and lead to suffering; true happiness lies in devotion to Kṛṣṇa.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this echoes the need to transcend the guṇas and realize the self as distinct from the body and mind, turning inward to the Supreme.

teachings.teaching: Association with saints (sat-saṅga) is the cause of devotion and liberation.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that kriya-yoga practice under a guru is the essential sat-saṅga that awakens bhakti.

teachings.teaching: One should not desire even liberation for oneself, but only service to the Lord.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with Sharma's view that the highest stage is niṣkāma bhakti, where action is offered without any personal motive.

teachings.teaching: Devotion to Kṛṣṇa purifies even the sins of a kṣatriya's hunting; one should fix the mind on Kṛṣṇa and perform one's duties.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga teaches that all actions, when performed with awareness of the Self and offered to the Divine, become yoga and burn past impressions.

chapter_summary: This chapter describes Krishna's marriage to Rukmini. It begins with the conclusion of the previous story (Mucukunda's departure), then narrates Krishna's return to Dvaraka, his encounter with Jarāsandha, the marriage of Balarama to Revati, and the main story of Rukmini's love for Krishna, her secret message sent via a Brahmana, and Krishna's plan to abduct her in the Rākṣasa style.

story.title: The Marriage of Krishna and Rukmini
story.arc: Krishna's Pastimes
story.timeline_note: After Mucukunda's departure, Krishna returns to Dvaraka, fights Jarāsandha, then marries Rukmini.
story.comic_potential: High: dramatic escape from fire, secret love letter, chariot abduction, battle with kings.

teachings.teaching: Contentment is the greatest wealth; even a poor contented man sleeps happily, while a discontented king suffers.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes santosha (contentment) as a key practice for inner peace and spiritual progress.

teachings.teaching: A brahmana who is content with whatever comes, steadfast in dharma, is the fulfiller of all desires.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga teaches that a yogi should be self-sufficient and not dependent on external circumstances.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is easily pleased by sincere devotion, not by mere rituals.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga stresses bhakti as the essence of yoga, surpassing external rites.

teachings.teaching: Rukmini's determination to marry Krishna despite opposition exemplifies surrender to the divine will.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga sees this as an example of complete self-surrender (prapatti) to the Lord.

chapter_summary: Krishna, having received Rukmini's message, decides to abduct her before her forced marriage to Shishupala. He travels to Kundina with a brahmana messenger. Meanwhile, Rukmini's father prepares for the wedding, and many enemy kings gather to prevent Krishna. Rukmini anxiously awaits Krishna, and the brahmana returns to reassure her. Krishna and Balarama arrive and are honored by the king. Rukmini goes to the temple of Ambika (Parvati) to pray, then leaves the temple, ready for Krishna to take her.

story.title: Rukmini's Prayer and Krishna's Arrival
story.arc: Krishna's marriage to Rukmini
story.timeline_note: This chapter occurs after Rukmini sends a message to Krishna and before the actual abduction at the temple.
story.comic_potential: Rukmini's anxious waiting, the brahmana's secret mission, the gathering of enemy kings, and the dramatic arrival of Krishna and Balarama provide high drama and tension.

teachings.teaching: Krishna reciprocates the love of his devotee and acts swiftly to protect them.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the principle of 'kriya' (action) arising from 'bhakti' (devotion). The devotee's longing (Rukmini's prayer) invokes the divine response (Krishna's action).

teachings.teaching: The divine plan unfolds despite obstacles created by enemies.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework emphasizes that obstacles are part of the divine play (lila) and serve to intensify the devotee's surrender. The gathering of hostile kings represents the ego's resistance to divine will.

teachings.teaching: Rukmini's prayer to Ambika exemplifies surrender to the divine feminine as the mediator.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, the goddess represents the energy (shakti) that activates the path. Rukmini's worship of Ambika symbolizes the devotee's invocation of inner power to unite with the divine.

chapter_summary: Rukmiṇī, adorned with jewels and a shy smile, walks gracefully to the temple, captivating the kings. She boards Krishna's chariot, and Krishna abducts her before the assembled monarchs, including Jarāsandha, who feel humiliated.

story.title: The Abduction of Rukmiṇī
story.arc: Krishna's marriage to Rukmiṇī
story.timeline_note: After Rukmiṇī's letter to Krishna, during the svayamvara.
story.comic_potential: High: dramatic entrance, kings fainting, swift chariot escape.

teachings.teaching: Divine love transcends worldly conventions; Krishna's abduction of Rukmiṇī is a pastime demonstrating his supremacy and the futility of opposing him.
teachings.lens_note: Illustrates the Lord's playful yet decisive intervention in the material world.

chapter_summary: This chapter describes the battle between Krishna's Yadava forces and the allied kings led by Jarasandha and Shishupala, who attempt to stop Krishna from abducting Rukmini. After defeating them, Krishna spares Rukmini's brother Rukmi at her request, but Balarama criticizes the leniency. Balarama then delivers a philosophical discourse on the nature of the self, illusion, and detachment.

story.title: The Battle with the Allied Kings and the Defeat of Rukmi
story.arc: Krishna's marriage to Rukmini
story.timeline_note: Immediately after Krishna abducts Rukmini from her svayamvara.
story.comic_potential: Rukmi's boastful challenge and subsequent humiliation (shaved head and mustache) provide comic relief.

teachings.teaching: The self (atman) is distinct from the body; attachment to the body causes suffering.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this aligns with the practice of disidentification from the body-mind complex to realize the true Self.

teachings.teaching: One should not grieve over the inevitable; the world is under the control of time and destiny.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes accepting the flow of karma and focusing on inner practice rather than external outcomes.

teachings.teaching: True knowledge dispels ignorance-born sorrow.
teachings.lens_note: This echoes the Kriya-Yoga goal of removing avidya through direct realization.

chapter_summary: After Rukmiṇī's abduction, Śiśupāla, humiliated and defeated by Kṛṣṇa, builds the city of Bhojakaṭa and vows not to enter Kuṇḍina until he kills Kṛṣṇa. Meanwhile, Kṛṣṇa marries Rukmiṇī in Dvārakā with great celebration, and the news astonishes neighboring kings.

story.title: Śiśupāla's Vow and Kṛṣṇa's Marriage to Rukmiṇī
story.arc: Kṛṣṇa's pastimes in Dvārakā
story.timeline_note: Immediately after Rukmiṇī's abduction from Kuṇḍina.
story.comic_potential: Śiśupāla's dramatic vow and the lavish wedding preparations in Dvārakā.

teachings.teaching: One should abandon mental distress and fix the mind with intelligence, even when facing defeat.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this reflects the practice of pratyāhāra (withdrawal of senses) and dhāraṇā (concentration) to overcome ego-driven reactions.

teachings.teaching: The Lord marries His devotee in accordance with Vedic rites, establishing dharma.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework emphasizes that divine actions (līlā) are models for human conduct; here, Kṛṣṇa's marriage exemplifies righteous union.

teachings.teaching: Celebration and joy arise from devotion to Kṛṣṇa, not from material opulence.
teachings.lens_note: The festival in Dvārakā illustrates the Kriya-Yoga principle that inner bliss (ānanda) is the result of union with the Divine.

chapter_summary: This chapter narrates the story of Pradyumna, the son of Krishna and Rukmini, who is an incarnation of Kamadeva. Kidnapped as an infant by the demon Shambara, he is thrown into the ocean, swallowed by a fish, and eventually recovered. Raised by Mayavati (Rati in disguise), he learns of his identity, defeats Shambara, and returns to Dvaraka, reuniting with his family.

story.title: The Return of Pradyumna
story.arc: Pradyumna's abduction, growth in the demon's house, and victorious return to Dvaraka
story.timeline_note: Occurs after Krishna's marriage to Rukmini and before the later exploits of Pradyumna.
story.comic_potential: High: infant thrown into sea, swallowed by fish, raised by demoness, identity reveal, battle with magical weapons, and dramatic family reunion.

teachings.teaching: The divine play (lila) of the Lord includes apparent loss and recovery, demonstrating that nothing is truly lost in the spiritual realm.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the principle of pratyahara (withdrawal of senses) and the eventual return to the source. The infant Pradyumna represents the soul temporarily separated from the divine, but through grace and inner knowledge (vidya), it reunites with its origin.

teachings.teaching: The power of divine knowledge (vidya) overcomes all illusions (maya).
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that the vidya given by Mayavati symbolizes the Kriya-Yoga technique that destroys the maya of worldly attachments. Pradyumna's victory over Shambara's myriad illusions represents the yogi's conquest of the mind's distractions.

chapter_summary: This chapter narrates the story of the Syamantaka gem. Satrajit, a devotee of the Sun god, receives the gem from Surya. His brother Prasena is killed by a lion while wearing the gem, and the lion is killed by Jambavan. Krishna is falsely accused of taking the gem. To clear his name, Krishna retrieves the gem from Jambavan's cave, fights Jambavan for 28 days, and is recognized as the Supreme Lord. Jambavan offers his daughter Jambavati and the gem to Krishna. Krishna returns the gem to Satrajit, who then gives his daughter Satyabhama and the gem to Krishna, but Krishna declines the gem.

story.title: The Syamantaka Gem
story.arc: Krishna clears his name of false accusation, retrieves the gem, and marries Satyabhama and Jambavati.
story.timeline_note: Occurs during Krishna's residence in Dvaraka, before the Kurukshetra war.
story.comic_potential: The mistaken identity of Satrajit as Surya, the lion and Jambavan fight, and the dramatic return from the cave.

teachings.teaching: The Lord clears the false accusations against him by revealing the truth.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the principle of satya (truthfulness) as a foundational practice; the yogi must purify their reputation through action, not just words.

teachings.teaching: Jambavan recognizes Krishna as the Supreme Lord after a fierce fight, showing that even the strong must surrender.
teachings.lens_note: This echoes the Kriya-Yoga teaching of Ishvara-pranidhana (surrender to God); the ego must be subdued through disciplined effort before realization dawns.

teachings.teaching: Krishna declines the Syamantaka gem, showing detachment from material wealth.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this represents vairagya (dispassion); the yogi should not be attached to even the most valuable objects, but use them for service.

chapter_summary: Krishna and Balarama visit Hastinapura after the Kuru fire, then return to Dvaraka. Satrajit is murdered by Shatadhanva for the Syamantaka jewel. Shatadhanva flees, is killed by Krishna, but the jewel is missing. Balarama goes to Mithila, learns gadā from Janaka. Krishna returns to Dvaraka, performs funeral rites. Akrura and Kritavarman flee. Later, Krishna summons Akrura, who returns the jewel. The chapter ends with a phalaśruti.

story.title: The Syamantaka Jewel: Murder and Return
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes in Dvaraka
story.timeline_note: After the Kuru fire incident, before the Mahabharata war.
story.comic_potential: High: murder mystery, chase, divine intervention, and a jewel that brings prosperity.

teachings.teaching: Even the Lord performs funeral rites for a devotee, showing the importance of dharma.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates that action (kriya) must be performed with detachment, as Krishna acts without personal motive.

teachings.teaching: The Lord knows the hearts of all; He tests His devotees.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework: The guru's omniscience is a mirror for the sadhaka to purify intentions.

teachings.teaching: One who hears this story attains peace and removes sin.
teachings.lens_note: The phalaśruti emphasizes the power of śravaṇa (hearing) in Kriya-Yoga as a means of purification.

chapter_summary: Krishna visits the Pandavas in Indraprastha, marries Kalindi (daughter of Surya), and later marries Mitravinda and Satya (Nagnajiti) after subduing seven bulls. The chapter also recounts Arjuna's burning of the Khandava forest and receiving divine weapons from Agni.

story.title: Krishna's Marriages and the Khandava Forest Episode
story.arc: Krishna's visit to Indraprastha and his marriages to Kalindi, Mitravinda, and Satya, interspersed with the burning of Khandava forest and Arjuna's receipt of divine weapons.
story.timeline_note: Occurs after the Pandavas have established Indraprastha, before the Rajasuya sacrifice and the dice game.
story.comic_potential: Krishna subduing seven fierce bulls with ease, and Arjuna's hunting spree in the forest.

teachings.teaching: Krishna is the supreme lord who removes the suffering of his devotees.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this reflects the principle that the inner Self (Atman) as Krishna removes the kleśas (afflictions) when one remembers him constantly.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is self-satisfied and needs nothing, yet he accepts the daughter of a king out of friendship.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes that the yogi, being self-fulfilled (ātmānandena pūrṇasya), acts out of compassion without personal need.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's pastimes are performed effortlessly (līlā).
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates that the supreme consciousness accomplishes everything with ease, like a child playing.

chapter_summary: Krishna marries several princesses: Satya (daughter of Nagnajit), Bhadra (daughter of Srutakirti), and Lakshmana (daughter of the Madra king). He also rescues many women from the demon Bhauma. The chapter describes the weddings and the defeat of opposing kings by Arjuna.

story.title: Krishna's Marriages to Satya, Bhadra, and Lakshmana
story.arc: Krishna's multiple marriages and rescue of captive women
story.timeline_note: Occurs after Krishna's establishment in Dvaraka, before the Kurukshetra war.
story.comic_potential: Arjuna's lion-like routing of kings and Krishna's swooping abduction of Lakshmana at her svayamvara.

teachings.teaching: Krishna's marriages demonstrate his divine pastimes and his role as the supreme enjoyer.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, these marriages symbolize the soul's union with the divine through devotion, where each queen represents a different aspect of bhakti.

teachings.teaching: Arjuna's protection of Krishna's wedding party shows the importance of serving the Lord's mission.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would see this as the active engagement of the devotee in protecting and furthering the divine will, a key aspect of Kriya-Yoga karma.

teachings.teaching: Krishna rescues the women from Bhauma, illustrating his compassion and deliverance of the suffering.
teachings.lens_note: This represents the Lord's grace in freeing souls from the bondage of material existence (Bhauma as demon of earthiness).

chapter_summary: King Parīkṣit asks Śuka about Kṛṣṇa's killing of Naraka and rescue of the captive princesses. Śuka narrates how Kṛṣṇa, informed by Indra of Naraka's theft of his earrings and other misdeeds, went to Prāgjyotiṣa with Satyabhāmā on Garuḍa. He broke through the fortifications, killed the demon Mura and his seven sons, then Naraka himself. Earth (Bhūmi) appeared, offered the earrings and other treasures, and pleaded for her son Naraka's forgiveness. Kṛṣṇa granted him liberation. He then entered Naraka's palace, found 16,000 captive princesses, and sent them to Dvārakā. He returned Indra's earrings, uprooted the pārijāta tree for Satyabhāmā, and later married all the princesses simultaneously by expanding into many forms.

story.title: The Killing of Naraka and the Rescue of the Princesses
story.arc: Kṛṣṇa's pastimes in Dvārakā
story.timeline_note: Occurs after Kṛṣṇa's establishment in Dvārakā, before the great war.
story.comic_potential: High: Kṛṣṇa's playful expansion into many forms to marry 16,000 princesses simultaneously, and the uprooting of the pārijāta tree causing a celestial commotion.

teachings.teaching: The Lord protects his devotees and punishes the wicked, as seen in Kṛṣṇa's response to Indra's plea.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the principle of 'Ishvara pranidhana' (surrender to God) leading to divine intervention.

teachings.teaching: Even a demon like Naraka can attain liberation by being killed by the Lord, emphasizing the Lord's mercy.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework sees this as the dissolution of ego (asura) through divine grace, a key step in Kriya-Yoga.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is the supreme controller and the ultimate refuge, as expressed in Bhūmi's prayers.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga concept of recognizing the one consciousness behind all manifestations.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's simultaneous marriage to many princesses demonstrates his transcendental ability to be with all devotees equally.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this symbolizes the soul's union with the divine in all aspects of life, a state of non-dual bliss.

chapter_summary: Krishna teases Rukmini by pretending to be unworthy of her, testing her devotion. Rukmini responds with a heartfelt defense of her choice, affirming Krishna as the supreme Lord. Krishna then consoles her, praising her unwavering love and explaining the nature of true devotion.

story.title: Krishna's Teasing and Rukmini's Devotion
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes in Dvaraka
story.timeline_note: After Krishna's marriage to Rukmini, early in their domestic life.
story.comic_potential: Krishna's playful teasing and Rukmini's dramatic reaction create a humorous yet tender scene.

teachings.teaching: True devotees are not attached to material wealth or status; they seek only the Lord.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: This teaching emphasizes the practice of inner detachment (vairagya) as essential for yoga. The devotee's focus on Krishna alone mirrors the yogi's single-pointed concentration on the Self.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is the supreme goal and fulfiller of all desires for his devotees.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: This aligns with the concept of Ishvara-pranidhana (surrender to God) as a key practice. The devotee's unwavering faith leads to ultimate fulfillment beyond material desires.

teachings.teaching: Material bodies are impure; only those who are attracted to Krishna's lotus feet are wise.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: This teaching underscores the need for discrimination (viveka) between the eternal Self and the temporary body, a foundational practice in Kriya-Yoga.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's playful words are meant to deepen the devotee's love and test their sincerity.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: This reflects the guru's method of using apparent obstacles to strengthen the disciple's resolve and devotion, akin to the challenges in yogic practice.

chapter_summary: In this chapter, Rukmini expresses her deep love and devotion to Krishna, recounting how she chose him over other kings and how he rescued her. She playfully chides him for his absence and his pastimes with other queens. Krishna responds with affectionate words, and the chapter concludes with a description of their intimate conversation and Krishna's domestic life.

story.title: Rukmini's Loving Complaint and Krishna's Response
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes in Dvaraka
story.timeline_note: After Krishna's marriage to Rukmini, during their domestic life in Dvaraka.
story.comic_potential: Rukmini's playful scolding and Krishna's affectionate replies create a light-hearted, intimate scene.

teachings.teaching: The Lord reciprocates the love of His devotees and engages in loving pastimes with them.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the principle of divine reciprocity: the practitioner's sincere devotion (bhakti) evokes a corresponding response from the divine, manifesting as inner guidance or grace.

teachings.teaching: Even the Supreme Lord, though self-satisfied, plays the role of a householder to teach dharma.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework emphasizes that the divine descends to demonstrate the ideal life of a householder, integrating spiritual practice with worldly duties.

chapter_summary: This chapter lists the sons of Krishna by his chief queens, describes the marriage of Aniruddha to Rocana (Rukmi's daughter), and narrates the dice game where Balarama defeats Rukmi and kills him after being insulted.

story.title: The Sons of Krishna and the Dice Game
story.arc: Genealogy and conflict resolution
story.timeline_note: After Krishna's marriages, his sons are born; later, Aniruddha marries Rocana; the dice game occurs at that wedding.
story.comic_potential: Balarama's rage and the dice game with kings could be depicted humorously, especially the tooth-breaking scene.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's queens and sons are described, showing the divine family's glory.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the principle of divine manifestation through pure sattvic relationships, where the Lord's pastimes inspire devotion.

teachings.teaching: Balarama's anger and killing of Rukmi shows that even great souls may act in wrath, but it is for upholding righteousness.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's commentary might interpret this as a lesson in controlled anger (krodha) used as a tool to protect dharma, not as a personal flaw.

chapter_summary: King Parīkṣit asks Śuka about the marriage of Aniruddha to Ūṣā, daughter of Bāṇa. Śuka narrates that Bāṇa, a devotee of Śiva, received a boon of a thousand arms and became arrogant. Śiva prophesied his pride would be crushed by a battle with someone equal to him. Meanwhile, Ūṣā dreamt of Aniruddha and fell in love. Her friend Citralekhā, a yoginī, identified Aniruddha and brought him from Dvārakā to Śoṇitapura. Ūṣā and Aniruddha lived together secretly. When discovered, Bāṇa attacked Aniruddha, who fought valiantly but was bound by Bāṇa's serpent ropes. Ūṣā lamented.

story.title: The Marriage of Aniruddha and Ūṣā
story.arc: Bāṇa's pride leads to conflict with Kṛṣṇa's family; divine intervention through love and battle.
story.timeline_note: Occurs after Kṛṣṇa's incarnation, during the time of Aniruddha's youth.
story.comic_potential: High: Secret romance, magical transport, battle with a thousand-armed demon, and a love-struck princess.

teachings.teaching: Pride leads to downfall; even a devotee of Śiva can become arrogant and face consequences.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, pride (ahaṅkāra) is a major obstacle to spiritual progress. Bāṇa's arrogance, despite his devotion, shows that ego can persist even in advanced practitioners. The battle symbolizes the inner struggle to surrender the ego.

teachings.teaching: Divine love transcends boundaries; Ūṣā's dream and Citralekhā's yogic powers bring lovers together.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, the soul's longing for the Divine is like Ūṣā's dream. Citralekhā's yoga represents the power of focused intention and divine grace to unite the individual self with the Supreme.

teachings.teaching: The righteous fight against oppression; Aniruddha defends himself against Bāṇa's forces.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, the battle represents the inner fight against negative tendencies. Aniruddha's courage symbolizes the soul's determination to uphold dharma despite overwhelming odds.

chapter_summary: Bāṇa, a demon with a thousand arms, imprisons Aniruddha. The Vṛṣṇis, led by Kṛṣṇa and Balarāma, attack Śoṇitapura. Śiva fights on Bāṇa's side. After a fierce battle, Kṛṣṇa cuts off Bāṇa's arms except four, spares him due to Prahlāda's boon and Śiva's request, and releases Aniruddha with his wife Uṣā.

story.title: The Liberation of Aniruddha and the Defeat of Bāṇāsura
story.arc: Kṛṣṇa's pastimes in Dvārakā
story.timeline_note: Occurs after Aniruddha is captured by Bāṇa; four months pass before the Vṛṣṇis attack.
story.comic_potential: High: cosmic battle with gods, demons, magical weapons, and a fever personified; dramatic rescues and divine interventions.

teachings.teaching: The Supreme Lord is the sole cause of creation, maintenance, and dissolution; all other entities are manifestations of His māyā.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this affirms that the ultimate reality is pure consciousness (jñapti-mātra), and all phenomena are its projections. The practitioner should recognize the world as a play of consciousness.

teachings.teaching: The Lord incarnates to protect dharma and relieve the earth's burden.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that divine incarnations demonstrate the path of dharma through action. The devotee should emulate this by engaging in righteous action without attachment.

teachings.teaching: One who neglects the Lord's lotus feet, having obtained a human birth, is pitiable and cheats himself.
teachings.lens_note: This echoes the Kriya-Yoga emphasis on human birth as a rare opportunity for self-realization. The practitioner must diligently practice meditation and devotion.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is the innermost Self and dearest friend; those who turn away from Him for sense enjoyment are like those who drink poison and abandon nectar.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma interprets this as a warning against identifying with the body and senses. Kriya-Yoga aims to turn the mind inward to experience the inner Self.

teachings.teaching: Even great beings like Brahmā and Śiva surrender to the Lord; humility and devotion are essential.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, surrender (prapatti) is a key practice. The ego must be offered to the divine to attain liberation.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's mercy extends even to demons; He cuts their pride but grants protection.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma sees this as an example of divine grace that transcends karma. The practitioner should cultivate compassion and see the Lord in all beings.

chapter_summary: After being blessed by Lord Shiva, Krishna returns to his capital Dvaraka with his wives, entering the city amidst festive decorations and sounds of conches and drums. The chapter concludes with a verse stating that one who recites this account of Krishna's victory over Shiva in the morning will never face defeat.

story.title: Krishna's Return to Dvaraka After Defeating Shiva
story.arc: Krishna's victory over Shiva and return to his capital
story.timeline_note: Immediately after the battle with Shiva described in the previous part of the chapter.
story.comic_potential: A grand procession with decorated streets, conches, and drums as Krishna enters Dvaraka with his wives.

teachings.teaching: Reciting the account of Krishna's victory over Shiva in the morning ensures one will never face defeat.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this teaching emphasizes the power of remembrance (smriti) as a practice to align with divine will and overcome obstacles. The morning recitation sets the tone for the day, invoking Krishna's victorious energy to neutralize karmic challenges.

chapter_summary: King Nṛga, a former king of the Ikṣvāku dynasty, recounts his fall from grace due to an inadvertent misdeed involving a Brahmin's cow. He was cursed to become a lizard for many years until rescued by Lord Kṛṣṇa. Kṛṣṇa then teaches the severe consequences of misappropriating Brahmin property.

story.title: The Deliverance of King Nṛga
story.arc: Kṛṣṇa's pastimes in Dvārakā
story.timeline_note: After Kṛṣṇa's return to Dvārakā, during his reign.
story.comic_potential: The scene of Yadu princes trying to rescue a giant lizard from a dry well, and Kṛṣṇa lifting it effortlessly, has visual humor. The dramatic revelation of the lizard as a former king adds pathos.

teachings.teaching: Misappropriation of Brahmin property, even unknowingly, leads to severe karmic consequences.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this underscores the principle of karma and the importance of purity in action (kriya). Even unintentional errors in dharma can bind one, emphasizing the need for vigilant awareness in every act.

teachings.teaching: The grace of the Lord can redeem even the most fallen, as seen when Kṛṣṇa rescues Nṛga.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga highlights that surrender to the divine (īśvara-praṇidhāna) is the ultimate means to overcome karmic bonds. Nṛga's devotion and remembrance of Kṛṣṇa even in his lizard form exemplify this.

chapter_summary: Balarama visits Nanda's Gokula, is warmly received by the gopas and gopis who inquire about Krishna. He consoles them with Krishna's messages, stays for two months, and enjoys pastimes with the gopis. He summons the Yamuna river, which initially disobeys, but then submits; Balarama drags her with his plow, then releases her upon her prayer. He sports in the water and returns adorned.

story.title: Balarama's Visit to Vraja and the Yamuna's Submission
story.arc: Balarama's pastimes in Vraja
story.timeline_note: After Krishna's return to Mathura, Balarama visits Gokula for two months (Madhu and Madhava months).
story.comic_potential: Balarama's drunken state, dragging the Yamuna river with his plow, and the river's personified plea create a vivid, humorous scene.

teachings.teaching: The gopis' intense love and separation from Krishna, and their willingness to abandon everything for him, exemplifies pure devotion.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this represents the soul's longing for union with the divine, where the gopis' lament is the practitioner's yearning for the inner Self.

teachings.teaching: Balarama's anger and subsequent mercy towards Yamuna shows that even divine beings can display human-like emotions, yet ultimately act for the benefit of devotees.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework sees this as the interplay of tamas (anger) and sattva (mercy) in the divine, teaching that all emotions can be sublimated into service.

teachings.teaching: Yamuna's submission and prayer highlight the power of surrender (prapatti) to the divine.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, surrender is the key to overcoming obstacles; the river's plea symbolizes the devotee's humility before the guru or divine will.

chapter_summary: Paundraka, the king of Karusha, deluded by his playmates into believing he is Vasudeva, sends a messenger to Krishna demanding he surrender his insignia. Krishna kills Paundraka and his ally the king of Kashi in battle. The king of Kashi's son Sudakshina performs a ritual to invoke a fiery demon, but Krishna's Sudarshana chakra destroys the demon and burns Varanasi.

story.title: The Killing of Paundraka and the Burning of Varanasi
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes as the supreme lord, demonstrating his supremacy over false claimants and protecting his devotees.
story.timeline_note: Occurs after Balarama's return to Nanda's village (bhp_10.65).
story.comic_potential: High; the absurdity of Paundraka's imitation of Krishna, the dramatic battle, and the fiery demon provide vivid visual and humorous elements.

teachings.teaching: False pride and delusion lead to destruction. Paundraka's claim to be Vasudeva out of ignorance results in his death.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the danger of ego (ahamkara) masquerading as divine identity. True self-realization requires surrender of the false self.

teachings.teaching: The Lord protects his devotees and destroys the wicked. Krishna's Sudarshana chakra annihilates the demon and burns Varanasi.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that divine grace (kriya) acts as a protective force; the chakra symbolizes the power of sustained practice that burns away impurities.

teachings.teaching: Even a demonic being who constantly meditates on Krishna attains liberation. Paundraka, though killed, becomes one with Krishna due to his constant thought of him.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with Sharma's teaching that intense focus (dhyana) on the divine, even with wrong motive, can purify the mind. The key is the quality of attention.

chapter_summary: King Parīkṣit asks Śuka about Balarāma's extraordinary deeds. Śuka narrates the story of Dvivida, a powerful monkey friend of Narakāsura, who wreaks havoc in the Yadu kingdom to avenge his friend. Dvivida disrupts sages, pollutes fire pits, imprisons people, and finally confronts Balarāma on Raivataka hill. After a fierce battle, Balarāma kills Dvivida with his fist, restoring peace.

story.title: The Slaying of Dvivida
story.arc: Balarāma's pastimes
story.timeline_note: After Narakāsura's death, Dvivida seeks revenge.
story.comic_potential: Dvivida's antics, including imitating Balarāma's wives and showing his rear, have slapstick humor.

teachings.teaching: The divine protects the righteous and destroys the wicked.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the principle of karma: actions have consequences, and divine intervention restores cosmic order.

teachings.teaching: Even a powerful demon cannot prevail against the Lord.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework emphasizes that ego and pride lead to downfall; surrender to the divine is the path to liberation.

chapter_summary: Samba, son of Krishna and Jambavati, abducts Lakshmana, daughter of Duryodhana, from her svayamvara. The Kauravas capture him. Balarama goes to Hastinapura to negotiate but is insulted. He becomes furious and drags the city of Hastinapura towards the Ganges with his plow. The Kauravas surrender and release Samba and Lakshmana, offering gifts. Balarama forgives them.

story.title: The Abduction of Lakshmana and Balarama's Wrath
story.arc: Balarama's intervention to free Samba and teach the Kauravas a lesson
story.timeline_note: Occurs after the events of the Mahabharata war? Actually, this is before the war, as Duryodhana is still alive and ruling.
story.comic_potential: Balarama dragging the entire city of Hastinapura with his plow is a visually humorous and exaggerated display of power.

teachings.teaching: Balarama's anger is for the sake of instructing the wicked, not out of hatred or envy.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates that divine anger is a teaching tool to correct adharma, not a personal emotion. The practitioner should use discernment to apply force when necessary for spiritual progress.

teachings.teaching: The Kauravas' pride in their material power leads to their downfall; humility and surrender bring protection.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework emphasizes that ego (ahamkara) is the root of bondage. The Kauravas' arrogance (verse 24-27) is a classic example of ego-driven action, which must be dissolved through surrender (prapatti) to the divine.

teachings.teaching: The Lord (as Balarama) is the sole support of all creation, yet remains detached.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga concept of the Self as the witness (sakshi) who is the substratum of all phenomena but untouched by them. The practitioner should cultivate this witness consciousness.

chapter_summary: Balarama (Halayudha) returns to Dvaraka after his pilgrimage, recounts his adventures to the Yadava assembly, and the city still bears marks of his prowess.

story.title: Balarama's Return to Dvaraka
story.arc: Balarama's pilgrimage and return
story.timeline_note: After the events of the pilgrimage described in previous chapters.
story.comic_potential: Balarama's triumphant return and the city's lasting marks of his strength.

teachings.teaching: The Lord accepts all offerings and returns with family, indicating the importance of hospitality and gratitude.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this reflects the principle of accepting grace (prasad) and returning to one's duties with renewed energy.

teachings.teaching: The city still shows signs of Balarama's past feats, symbolizing that divine actions leave lasting impressions.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes that spiritual practices leave subtle impressions (samskaras) that shape one's environment and consciousness.

chapter_summary: Narada visits Dvaraka to see how Krishna simultaneously lives with his 16,000 wives. He witnesses Krishna in different palaces engaging in various activities, demonstrating his yoga-maya. Narada praises Krishna and departs, and the chapter concludes with the glory of hearing this pastime.

story.title: Narada's Visit to Dvaraka: Seeing Krishna's Yoga-Maya
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes in Dvaraka
story.timeline_note: After Narada hears of Krishna's marriage to 16,000 wives and the slaying of Narakasura.
story.comic_potential: Narada's bewildered expression as he sees Krishna in multiple places at once, each engaged in different activities, could be played for humor.

teachings.teaching: Krishna, though the Supreme Lord, acts like a human to teach dharma to householders.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates that the yogi, while established in the Self, can engage in worldly activities without attachment, serving as a model for balanced living.

teachings.teaching: Krishna's simultaneous presence in many homes demonstrates his yoga-maya, the inconceivable power of the Lord.
teachings.lens_note: This shows that the divine can manifest in multiple forms simultaneously, a concept that in Kriya-Yoga corresponds to the realization of the omnipresent Self.

teachings.teaching: Hearing and chanting Krishna's pastimes with devotion leads to bhakti and liberation.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the power of satsang and nāma-japa as means to purify the mind and attain union with the divine.

chapter_summary: This chapter describes Krishna's daily routine in Dvaraka, his morning activities including meditation on Brahman, worship, and charity. He then goes to the Sudharma assembly hall, where he is entertained by dancers and musicians. A messenger from imprisoned kings arrives, pleading for Krishna's help against Jarāsandha. Nārada appears and informs Krishna about Yudhiṣṭhira's plan to perform the Rājasūya sacrifice. Krishna consults Uddhava.

story.title: Krishna's Daily Routine and the Imprisoned Kings' Plea
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes in Dvaraka; preparation for the Rājasūya sacrifice
story.timeline_note: Set after Krishna's establishment in Dvaraka, before the Rājasūya sacrifice.
story.comic_potential: The contrast between Krishna's serene morning routine and the dramatic plea of the imprisoned kings, followed by Nārada's sudden appearance, offers dramatic and humorous potential.

teachings.teaching: Krishna meditates on the Supreme Brahman, the self-luminous, unchanging reality beyond the three modes of nature.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this represents the practitioner's daily practice of samādhi, merging individual consciousness with the universal consciousness.

teachings.teaching: One should perform daily duties (sandhyā, agnihotra, japa) with full awareness, as Krishna does.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that Kriya-Yoga includes disciplined daily rituals (kriyā) to purify the mind and body, preparing for higher meditation.

teachings.teaching: Charity to brahmins, cows, and the needy is an essential part of spiritual life.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, selfless service (seva) is a key component, purifying the heart and reducing ego.

teachings.teaching: The kings lament that worldly happiness is dream-like and dependent on others; true happiness lies in the self, attainable through devotion to Krishna.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga teaches that lasting bliss comes from inner realization (ātma-sukha) through meditation, not from external objects.

teachings.teaching: Hearing, chanting, and meditating on Krishna's glories purify even the lowest.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with Kriya-Yoga's emphasis on nāma-japa and meditation as powerful purificatory practices.

chapter_summary: Uddhava advises Krishna to kill Jarāsandha to enable the Rājasūya sacrifice, and Krishna agrees. The Yādava army travels to Indraprastha, where Yudhiṣṭhira and the Pāṇḍavas welcome them. Krishna stays for several months, enjoying pastimes with Arjuna.

story.title: Uddhava's Advice and Krishna's Journey to Indraprastha
story.arc: Preparation for Rājasūya
story.timeline_note: After Nārada's visit, Uddhava speaks; then Krishna travels to Indraprastha and stays several months.
story.comic_potential: The grand procession of the Yādava army and the emotional reunion of Krishna with the Pāṇḍavas.

teachings.teaching: Killing a powerful enemy like Jarāsandha is necessary for a great sacrifice and for the welfare of many.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this represents the need to overcome internal obstacles (Jarāsandha as ego) to achieve higher spiritual goals (Rājasūya as self-realization).

teachings.teaching: The Lord is the ultimate cause of creation and destruction, manifesting as Brahmā and Śiva.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this highlights the unity of all divine functions in the one Supreme Consciousness.

teachings.teaching: Devotees sing of the Lord's deeds, including the killing of enemies and liberation of souls.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, singing and remembering the Lord's pastimes purifies the mind and leads to inner stillness.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's arrival brings joy and liberation to all who see Him.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, the vision of the Lord (darshan) is a transformative experience that dissolves karmic bonds.

teachings.teaching: Proper respect and hospitality should be shown to the Lord and His devotees.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, serving the Lord and His devotees is a form of yoga that purifies the heart.

chapter_summary: Yudhiṣṭhira expresses his desire to perform the Rājasūya sacrifice. Kṛṣṇa advises him to first conquer all kings. Yudhiṣṭhira sends his brothers to conquer the directions. They bring tribute. Kṛṣṇa, Arjuna, and Bhīma go to Girivraja disguised as brāhmaṇas to confront Jarāsandha. After a fierce mace fight, Bhīma splits Jarāsandha's body. Kṛṣṇa installs Jarāsandha's son Sahadeva as king and releases the imprisoned kings.

story.title: The Slaying of Jarāsandha and the Prelude to the Rājasūya
story.arc: Yudhiṣṭhira's Rājasūya preparation
story.timeline_note: After the Pāṇḍavas' exile and before the great war.
story.comic_potential: High: Disguised heroes, a boastful villain, a dramatic mace fight, and a gruesome split.

teachings.teaching: The Rājasūya sacrifice is the king of sacrifices and purifies the performer.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this symbolizes the culmination of self-discipline and service, where the purified self (king) offers all actions to the Divine.

teachings.teaching: One should conquer the senses (kings) before performing great deeds.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes conquering the inner enemies (desire, anger, etc.) as a prerequisite for spiritual progress.

teachings.teaching: The body is impermanent; use it to attain eternal fame through righteous acts.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga views the body as a temporary instrument for yoga practice to achieve liberation.

teachings.teaching: Even a fallen king like Bali gave everything to a brāhmaṇa, showing the value of charity.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the principle of surrendering the ego (the kingdom) to the higher self (brāhmaṇa).

chapter_summary: Krishna releases the kings imprisoned by Jarasandha, who praise Krishna and renounce worldly desires.
