teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes that consistent practice (kriya) and devotion purify the mind, making one receptive to divine grace.

teachings.teaching: The entire universe is a manifestation of the Lord's māyā, and He contains all within Himself.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework interprets this as the realization that the macrocosm and microcosm are one; through yoga, one can perceive the divine in all.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's form as a child on a banyan leaf symbolizes His transcendence and immanence.
teachings.lens_note: This illustrates the Kriya-Yoga principle that the divine is both beyond creation and intimately present within it, accessible through inner meditation.

chapter_summary: Markandeya Rishi, after experiencing the illusion created by Narayana, seeks refuge in the Lord. Lord Shiva and Parvati visit him, and Shiva grants him boons including unalloyed devotion to Vishnu, eternal fame, and knowledge. The chapter extols the power of the Lord's maya and the importance of devotion.

story.title: Markandeya's Vision and Shiva's Blessing
story.arc: Markandeya Rishi, after experiencing the cosmic illusion of Narayana, is visited by Lord Shiva and Parvati. Shiva praises the sage and grants him boons, including unalloyed devotion to Vishnu, eternal fame, and knowledge.
story.timeline_note: After the events of the previous chapter where Markandeya experienced Narayana's maya.
story.comic_potential: The sudden appearance of Shiva and Parvati before the meditating sage, and the sage's awe at their divine forms, offers dramatic visual potential.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's maya confuses even the wise, but taking refuge in Him is the ultimate shelter.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to surrendering the ego to the divine will, recognizing that all experiences are part of the cosmic play.

teachings.teaching: Association with saints is the highest gain for humans.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes satsang (holy company) as a key practice for spiritual progress.

teachings.teaching: The sage, though capable of receiving boons, desires only devotion to the Lord.
teachings.lens_note: This illustrates the Kriya-Yoga ideal of desirelessness and single-pointed devotion.

teachings.teaching: Brahmins who are devoted to the Lord are honored even by the gods.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, the inner brahmin (the realized self) is revered as the highest.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's maya is inscrutable; even the gods bow to His devotees.
teachings.lens_note: This reinforces the Kriya-Yoga teaching that grace descends through humility and devotion.

chapter_summary: This chapter describes the cosmic form of the Supreme Lord (Mahā-puruṣa) as the source of the universe, detailing how the various parts of the universe correspond to His body. It then explains the worship of the Lord through the sun's course, listing the deities presiding over each month, and concludes with the benefits of remembering these manifestations.

story.title: Chapter 11
story.arc: This chapter describes the cosmic form of the Supreme Lord (Mahā-puruṣa) as the source of the universe, detailing how the various parts of the universe correspond to His body. It then explains the worship of the Lord through the sun's course, listing the deities presiding over each month, and concludes with the benefits of remembering these manifestations.

chapter_summary: Sūta summarizes the entire Bhāgavata Purāṇa, listing its contents from creation to the life of Kṛṣṇa and the Kali-yuga. He extols the glory of hearing and chanting the Lord's pastimes, which purify the heart and lead to liberation.

story.title: Chapter 12 (part 1)
story.arc: Sūta summarizes the entire Bhāgavata Purāṇa, listing its contents from creation to the life of Kṛṣṇa and the Kali-yuga. He extols the glory of hearing and chanting the Lord's pastimes, which purify the heart and lead to liberation.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Bhagavata Purana (Chapter 12, part 2) extols the glories of Lord Vasudeva (Krishna) and the benefits of reciting or hearing the Purana. It emphasizes that constant remembrance of Krishna's lotus feet destroys impurities and bestows purity, devotion, knowledge, and detachment. The text also describes the rewards for different classes of people who study or hear the Purana, and concludes with a prayer to the Supreme Lord and a salutation to Vyasa's son (Suta Goswami).

story.title: The Glories of the Bhagavata Purana
story.arc: Concluding instructions and benedictions
story.timeline_note: After the narration of the Purana to King Parikshit
story.comic_potential: A sage listing rewards for recitation, with visual metaphors of rivers of honey, ghee, and milk.

teachings.teaching: Constant remembrance of Krishna's lotus feet destroys all impurities and bestows auspiciousness, purity of mind, devotion to the Supreme, knowledge, and detachment.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to the practice of constant awareness of the divine (Ishvara-pranidhana), which purifies the mind and leads to self-realization.

teachings.teaching: Hearing or reciting even a single verse of the Bhagavata Purana with faith purifies the soul.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes the power of sound and mantra in Kriya-Yoga; the sacred syllables of the Purana act as a purifying vibration.

teachings.teaching: Reciting the Purana on specific days (dvadashi, ekadashi) or at sacred places (Pushkara, Mathura, Dvaraka) grants longevity and liberation from fear.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga practice of aligning actions with auspicious times and places to enhance spiritual efficacy.

teachings.teaching: The Purana is the supreme light of truth, given by Vyasa's son out of compassion, destroying all sins.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma views the Purana as a direct transmission of divine knowledge (jnana) that, when received with humility, removes ignorance.

chapter_summary: This chapter enumerates the eighteen major Purāṇas with their verse counts, extols the Śrīmad Bhāgavatam as the supreme Purāṇa, describes its origin from Lord Viṣṇu to Brahmā, and prescribes the merit of donating and reciting it. It concludes with prayers to the Lord and Śuka.

story.title: The Glorification of the Bhāgavata Purāṇa
story.arc: Concluding praise of the Bhāgavata as the essence of all Purāṇas and Vedānta, with instructions on its donation and recitation.
story.timeline_note: Post-Kurukshetra, during the recitation of the Bhāgavata by Sūta to the sages at Naimiṣāraṇya.
story.comic_potential: The vivid imagery of the cosmic turtle (Kūrma) scratching its back on Mandara mountain, causing ocean tides, could be depicted humorously.

teachings.teaching: The Bhāgavata Purāṇa is the essence of all Vedānta, leading to liberation through knowledge, detachment, and devotion.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to the integration of jñāna (knowledge), vairāgya (detachment), and bhakti (devotion) as the three pillars of spiritual practice, culminating in naiṣkarmya (actionless state).

teachings.teaching: Donating a copy of the Bhāgavata on the full moon of Prauṣṭhapada (Bhādrapada) with a golden lion leads to the supreme goal.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma might interpret this as a symbolic act of offering one's ego (lion) and material wealth (gold) to the divine, aligning with Kriya-Yoga's emphasis on self-surrender.

teachings.teaching: Hearing, reciting, and contemplating the Bhāgavata with devotion liberates one from bondage.
teachings.lens_note: This echoes Kriya-Yoga's practice of śravaṇa (hearing), manana (contemplation), and nididhyāsana (meditation) on the scriptures as a means to realize the self.
